title: The Toast: Four Patterns in Every Glass
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can name the four patterns that run through every drink in Season 2.
Subject: Season synthesis | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome to the last episode of Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. This is Anthony McDonald. We have followed six drinks: beer, wine, whiskey, rum, the agave spirits, and sake. This episode does something different. Instead of one drink, we look back at all of them at once, because the same four patterns show up in every single glass.

[HOOK] Here is the honest note one more time, and it matters most at the end. We have studied these drinks as history, geography, economics, and culture, and we have been straight the whole way about the harm alcohol does. Raising a glass in this class has always meant raising a question. Now the hook: once you have seen six drinks, the patterns stop hiding. The same forces shaped a Sumerian beer and a bottle of Japanese sake four thousand years apart.

[TEACH] There are four patterns, one for each lens. Pattern one, geography insists: the ground speaks first, and geology, water, and climate decide what a place can make. Pattern two, fermentation begins sacred: nearly every drink started in a temple or a ceremony, then was commercialized, and harm rose as communal care fell away. Pattern three, labor captures the smallest share: the people who do the hardest physical work earn the least, a shape economists call the smile curve. Pattern four, whose story survives: there is always a marketing story and a suppressed one, and the power to decide which is remembered is never neutral. Each lesson takes one pattern across the whole season, and we end by raising a glass in ten languages.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. The four patterns are: geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives. They are the spine of the whole season, and once you see them in a drink, you start seeing them everywhere else too.

[CHECK] Quick check. Name two of the four season-wide patterns. [Beat] Any two of: geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives.

## Key Takeaways
- This finale synthesizes all six Season 2 drinks into four cross-season patterns.
- The four patterns: geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives.
- The season has been honest about alcohol's harms throughout; this is education, not an endorsement.
- The episode ends by examining the toast itself, in ten languages.

## Sources
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.

title: Pattern 1: The Ground Speaks First
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can state the universal terroir rule that connects every Season 2 drink.
Subject: Geography synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we named the four patterns. Two quick questions.
One: name two of the four patterns. [Beat] Any two of geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives.
Two: how does the season end? [Beat] By examining the toast in ten languages.

[HOOK] Across this whole season, one geographic rule never broke. From a Czech lager to a Japanese sake, the same chain held in every glass. If you remember one idea from all of Season 2's geography, make it this one.

[TEACH] The rule is: geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, and fermentation shapes the drink. The ground speaks first. Look at the evidence we gathered. In Plzen, soft sandstone water made a pale, crisp Pilsner; in Dublin, hard limestone water made dark stout; in Burton, gypsum water full of sulfate made bitter IPA. In Kentucky, iron-free karst limestone water made smooth bourbon. And in Japan, three towns made three sakes from three waters: Nada's hard, mineral-rich water made a bold sake, Fushimi's soft water an elegant one, and Niigata's snowmelt a clean, dry one (Gautier, 2018). The minerals differ from place to place. The mechanism is always the same.
And it is not only water. Where the crop grows matters just as much. Barley and grapes and agave all make better drinks under stress, in poor soil or thin mountain air, while rice wants rich, wet comfort. So the universal terroir statement for the whole season is simple: the physical ground, its rock, water, soil, and climate, decides what a place can make and how it will taste. This is the same lesson Season 1 taught with coffee on volcanic slopes and tea at altitude. The ground always speaks first.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Eight pins, from a Czech town to a snowy Japanese coast, each one a different drink born from a different water. Picture the rock underneath each pin, quietly deciding the flavor in the glass above it. One rule, drawn across the whole world.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: across all of Season 2, geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, and fermentation shapes the drink, so the ground speaks first, the same rule in Czech sandstone, Irish limestone, Kentucky karst, and Japanese granite (Gautier, 2018). Terroir is universal.

[CHECK] Quick check. State the season's universal terroir rule in one sentence. [Beat] The physical ground, rock, water, soil, and climate, shapes the water and the crop, which shape the fermentation, which shapes the drink (Gautier, 2018).

## Key Takeaways
- The season's universal rule: geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, fermentation shapes the drink.
- Plzen, Dublin, Burton, Kentucky, and Japan's Nada, Fushimi, and Niigata all show the same mechanism with different minerals (Gautier, 2018).
- Crops add to it: barley, grapes, and agave thrive on stress, while rice wants comfort.
- The ground speaks first, the same lesson Season 1 taught with coffee and tea.

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.

title: Pattern 1, Continued: Stress and Care
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the spectrum from stress crops to comfort crops across the season.
Subject: Geography synthesis | Run time: about 5 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we stated the universal terroir rule. Two quick questions.
One: what speaks first, across the whole season? [Beat] The ground, geology shapes water, water shapes the drink (Gautier, 2018).
Two: which town's snowmelt water made a clean, dry sake? [Beat] Niigata (Gautier, 2018).

[HOOK] There is a second half to the geography pattern, and it is almost the opposite of the first. The water rule is about where a drink is made. This one is about how the plant is treated, and the drinks fall on a surprising spectrum from hardship to comfort.

[TEACH] Most of the season's plants make their best drinks under stress. Barley, beer's grain, prefers poor soil and cool nights in the temperate belt. The wine grape needs a little struggle, dry summers and well-drained, stony ground, to build complexity. And agave is the extreme case, a desert plant that turns drought and heat into concentrated sugar, the most stress-loving crop we met. For these plants, an easy life makes a boring drink.
But sake breaks the pattern, and that is the point. Rice is an aquatic crop that wants the opposite of stress: rich, water-retentive soil and a flooded paddy, plenty of water and care (Gautier, 2018). So the season's crops form a spectrum. At one end, stress specialists like agave. At the other, comfort crops like rice. And yet the deeper rule still holds for both, because what each plant needs is set by its geography, and matching the plant to its right ground is what makes a great drink. Whether a crop wants hardship or comfort, the ground still decides.

[PICTURE] Picture a line. On the far left, an agave baking on a dry volcanic hillside, thriving on hardship. On the far right, rice standing in a flooded green paddy, thriving on water and care. Beer's barley and wine's grape sit toward the stress end. The same season holds both extremes, and geography sets each one's place on the line.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the season's crops run on a spectrum from stress specialists, agave, barley, and the wine grape that thrive on hardship, to comfort crops like rice that want rich soil and water, but for all of them geography decides what they need (Gautier, 2018). Stress or care, the ground still rules.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which Season 2 crop wants comfort rather than stress, and what does it need? [Beat] Rice, the source of sake, which wants rich, water-retentive soil and a flooded paddy (Gautier, 2018).

## Key Takeaways
- Most season crops, barley, the wine grape, and especially agave, make better drinks under stress.
- Agave is the extreme stress specialist, turning drought and heat into concentrated sugar.
- Rice breaks the pattern, wanting rich, wet, comfortable conditions for sake.
- The crops form a stress-to-comfort spectrum, but geography still sets what each one needs (Gautier, 2018).

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.

title: Pattern 2: Fermentation Begins Sacred
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the season-wide pattern that fermented drinks start sacred, then turn commercial, and harm rises as that shift happens.
Subject: Social Studies synthesis | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we walked the stress-to-comfort spectrum. Two quick questions.
One: which Season 2 crop wants comfort rather than stress? [Beat] Rice, the source of sake, which wants rich, water-retentive soil and a flooded paddy (Gautier, 2018).
Two: what does the stress-to-comfort spectrum measure? [Beat] How each plant is treated, from stress specialists like agave at one end to comfort crops like rice at the other.

[HOOK] Here is the part of the research that surprised the host most. Go down the list of every drink in Season 2, and almost all of them begin in the same place. Not a market. Not a factory. A temple, a shrine, or a religious law. Fermentation begins sacred.

[TEACH] Start with the evidence, one drink at a time. Beer's oldest known text is the Sumerian Hymn to Ninkasi, a song to the goddess of brewing that doubled as a recipe, dated to about 1800 BCE (Civil, 1964). Wine carried the cult of Dionysus through the Mediterranean and became the Christian Eucharist, the cup at the center of the sacrament. Whiskey was named by Irish monks who called it the water of life, building on Arab alchemists who gave us the very word alcohol. Tequila and mezcal trace back to the Aztec goddess Mayahuel, and to pulque, a drink so sacred it was governed by religious law. Sake was prepared for Shinto kami, the spirits, and offered as o-miki, sacred drink, at the shrine.
There is one exception, and it proves the pattern. Rum had no sacred origin. It was a colonial product from the start, born on the sugar plantation to turn waste molasses into profit. Every drink with deep roots began as sacred. The one that began as commerce is the one with no temple behind it.
Now notice what those sacred systems did. They were not only ceremony. They were rules. The Code of Hammurabi set out beer regulations almost four thousand years ago. Aztec law made unauthorized public drunkenness a crime punishable by death, not because the Aztecs were against drinking, but because they treated intoxication as sacred ground that the community had to govern. Shinto practice tied sake to certain seasons and certain occasions. Each culture built accountability right into the act of drinking: who could drink, when, how much, and in front of whom.
And here is the arc the whole season traces. Sacred, then regulated, then commercialized. The drink starts holy. The community wraps rules around it. Then a market buys it, sells it, and strips the rules away to sell more. The communal accountability that once held the drink in check gets removed and replaced with a price tag. In case after case across the season, that removal is the moment harm rises. Not because the sacred was magic, but because it carried a working set of brakes that the market threw out.

[PICTURE] Picture six glasses on a table. Five of them sit on top of an old document: a clay tablet hymn, a law code, a monk's manuscript, a temple scroll. Each glass rests on the sacred rule that once governed it. The sixth glass, the rum, sits on a bare plantation ledger. Same table, two very different foundations.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: nearly every Season 2 drink began sacred, beer in the Hymn to Ninkasi (Civil, 1964), wine in Dionysus and the Eucharist, whiskey in monastic and Arab tradition, tequila and mezcal under the goddess Mayahuel, and sake as Shinto o-miki, with rum the lone colonial exception. The arc runs sacred, then regulated, then commercial, and harm rises as communal accountability is removed.

[CHECK] Quick check. State the sacred-to-commercial arc and name the one drink that skipped the start of it. [Beat] The arc runs sacred, then regulated, then commercialized, with harm rising as communal accountability is stripped away; rum is the exception, a colonial product with no sacred origin.

## Key Takeaways
- Almost every Season 2 drink has a documented sacred origin, beer in the Hymn to Ninkasi (Civil, 1964), wine, whiskey, tequila, mezcal, and sake all included.
- Rum is the exception that proves the rule, a colonial product with no pre-colonial sacred origin.
- Those sacred systems built rules into drinking: the Code of Hammurabi, the Aztec death penalty for unauthorized drunkenness, and Shinto seasonal restriction.
- The pattern's arc is sacred, then regulated, then commercialized, and harm rises as communal accountability is removed and replaced by market sale.

## Sources
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). University of Chicago Press.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.

title: Pattern 2, Continued: What Was Lost and What Came Back
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how colonialism disrupted fermentation traditions across both seasons, and what made some of them recoverable.
Subject: Social Studies synthesis | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we named the sacred-to-commercial pattern. Two quick questions.
One: what is the sacred-to-commercial arc? [Beat] Drinks begin sacred, then become regulated, then commercialized, and harm rises as communal accountability is removed (Civil, 1964).
Two: which drink was the exception with no sacred origin? [Beat] Rum, a colonial product from the start.

[HOOK] If sacred systems held drinking in check, then what happened when an outside power smashed those systems on purpose? That is the dark turn in the story, and it shows up again and again. Colonialism did not just take the land and the labor. It went after the knowledge and the records too.

[TEACH] Walk the inventory of what was disrupted. In South Africa, the dop system paid wine-farm workers in alcohol instead of wages, building chemical dependency right into the labor arrangement and leaving a legacy of harm for generations (London, 1999). In Mexico, Spanish colonizers burned Aztec codices, the painted books that held history, law, and the rules governing drink, until fewer than twenty pre-Columbian examples survive today (Leon-Portilla, 2002). When those books burned, the proof that Indigenous societies had sophisticated systems of their own burned with them. In Korea, Japanese occupation between 1910 and 1945 suppressed makgeolli, restricting the nuruk starter cultures that gave the rice wine its life and imposing Japanese sake methods in their place (Park & Kim, 2019). Three cultures, three mechanisms: wages turned into alcohol, books turned into ash, living cultures nearly starved out.
But the story does not end at loss, and that is the point. Some of it came back. Since the 2000s, Korean brewers and food scientists have rebuilt the nuruk cultures, reviving them as a source of cultural identity (Park & Kim, 2019). In Mexico, the maestro mezcalero tradition has been revived by the families who kept the craft alive. And the histories of Maroon communities, self-freed people who built their own societies, survived through oral tradition and were later set down by scholars. Yet some loss is permanent. Most of the content of those burned codices is simply gone, and no revival can bring it back (Leon-Portilla, 2002).
So here is the question worth sitting with: what made some traditions recoverable when others were lost for good? Look at what the recovered ones share. First, community memory stayed alive, an elder who still knew the nuruk, a family that still ran the still, a people who still told the story out loud. Second, someone with a platform finally documented it, a scientist, a scholar, a writer who could carry the memory beyond the few who held it. Where both held, recovery was possible. Where the memory was severed and no one wrote it down, the loss became permanent.

[PICTURE] Picture two shelves. On the top shelf, three things that came back: a jar of living nuruk starter, a bottle of revived mezcal, and a printed book of Maroon history. On the bottom shelf, a single charred fragment of a codex, the only piece left of thousands. The top shelf survived because someone remembered and someone wrote it down. The bottom shelf is what happens when neither does.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: colonialism disrupted fermentation across both seasons, from the South African dop system paying workers in wine (London, 1999), to the burning of Aztec codices down to fewer than twenty survivors (Leon-Portilla, 2002), to the suppression of Korean makgeolli and its nuruk cultures (Park & Kim, 2019). Some came back because community memory stayed alive and someone with a platform recorded it. Some loss, like most of the codex content, is permanent.

[CHECK] Quick check. What two conditions made some disrupted traditions recoverable? [Beat] Community memory kept alive, an elder, family, or people who still held the practice, and someone with a platform finally documenting it so the memory could outlast the few who carried it (Park & Kim, 2019).

## Key Takeaways
- Colonialism disrupted fermentation across both seasons: the South African dop system (London, 1999), burned Aztec codices (Leon-Portilla, 2002), and suppressed Korean makgeolli (Park & Kim, 2019).
- The mechanisms differed: wages paid in alcohol, books burned to ash, and living starter cultures nearly starved out.
- Some traditions recovered, the Korean nuruk revival since the 2000s, the maestro mezcalero revival, and the documented Maroon histories.
- Some loss is permanent, including most of the burned codex content (Leon-Portilla, 2002).
- Recovery depended on two things: community memory kept alive, and someone with a platform finally documenting it.

## Sources
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). University of Chicago Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (2002). The broken spears: The Aztec account of the conquest of Mexico (Rev. ed.). Beacon Press.
- London, L. (1999). The dop system, alcohol abuse and social control amongst farm workers in South Africa. Social Science & Medicine, 48(10), 1407-1414.
- Park, S. J., & Kim, Y. H. (2019). The revival of traditional Korean rice wine makgeolli. Journal of Ethnic Foods, 6(1), 1-9.

title: Pattern 3: Labor Captures the Smallest Share
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the smile curve and show how it placed the season's hardest workers at the bottom.
Subject: Economics synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we asked what was lost and what came back in the season's fermentation traditions. Two quick questions.
One: name one colonial power that disrupted a fermentation tradition, and the tradition it hit. [Beat] Japanese colonial authorities suppressed Korean makgeolli brewing between 1910 and 1945; or Spanish colonizers burned Aztec codices that held pulque law.
Two: what made some traditions recoverable when others were not? [Beat] Surviving knowledge, an oral tradition or a community still practicing it, gave people something to rebuild from, as with the Korean nuruk revival and Maroon oral histories.

[HOOK] Across this whole season, the same uncomfortable shape kept showing up in the money. The person whose body actually touches the raw material, who harvests, who ferments, who ages it, takes home the smallest slice of the price on the shelf. Economists drew that shape, and it looks like a smile.

[TEACH] The idea comes from the economist Ravi Mudambi. In a supply chain, value capture is high at the two ends and low in the middle (Mudambi, 2008). At the start sits research, design, and branding. At the end sits marketing, distribution, and retail. In between sits the physical work, the farming, the production, the manufacturing. Plot how much value each step captures and the line dips low in the center and rises at both edges, so it looks like a smile.
Beverages make that smile vivid, because the labor is so physical and the products are so premium. Walk the bottom of the curve with the season's workers. The jimador who harvests agave in the Mexican sun earns about fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day (Bowen, 2015). The Caribbean sugarcane cutter, swinging a blade in tropical heat, earns about ten to twenty dollars a day (Beckford and Campbell, 2013). Even the Kentucky rickhouse worker, who hauls heavy barrels through an aging warehouse, earns about one hundred twenty to one hundred eighty dollars a day. All three sit at the bottom. Meanwhile the celebrity whose name is on the bottle sits at the top, capturing the brand premium on a spirit that can retail for fifty to three hundred dollars (Bowen, 2015).
Here is the sharp part. The jimador and the rickhouse worker earn very different wages, one paid roughly ten times more than the other. Yet both sit at the bottom. How? Because the curve is not about how many dollars you take home. It is about your share of the value, your slice of where the price goes. Both do the irreplaceable physical work, and both capture a small share of the price while branding and marketing capture the large one. Pay is the level you stand at. Share is where you sit on the smile. The two are not the same.

[PICTURE] Picture a wide smile drawn across a page. At the left corner, high up, a brand designer. At the right corner, high up, a shelf in a liquor store. And sagging across the whole bottom, in the dip of the smile, the jimador with a coa blade, the cane cutter, and the rickhouse worker with a barrel on his shoulder. Different wages, same low seat. The smile is friendly. The shape is not.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Mudambi's smile curve shows value capture is high at the two ends, branding at the start and marketing at the end, and lowest in the physical middle (Mudambi, 2008). In beverages the jimador at about fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day (Bowen, 2015) and the cane cutter at about ten to twenty dollars a day (Beckford and Campbell, 2013) both sit at the bottom, because the curve measures share of value, not absolute pay.

[CHECK] Quick check. The jimador earns far less than a Kentucky rickhouse worker, yet both sit at the bottom of the smile curve. Why? [Beat] Because the curve measures share of the final value captured, not the size of the wage; both do physical middle-of-the-chain work that takes a small slice of the price, while branding and marketing take the large one (Mudambi, 2008; Bowen, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- The smile curve maps value capture: high at branding at the start and marketing at the end, low in the physical middle (Mudambi, 2008).
- Beverages show it plainly because the work is physical and the products are premium.
- The jimador (about fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day), the Caribbean cane cutter (about ten to twenty dollars a day), and the rickhouse worker (about one hundred twenty to one hundred eighty dollars a day) all sit at the bottom (Bowen, 2015; Beckford and Campbell, 2013).
- The curve is about share of value, not absolute pay, so different wages can share the same low seat.

## Sources
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Beckford, G., & Campbell, M. (2013). Persistent poverty: Underdevelopment in plantation economies of the Third World. University of the West Indies Press.

title: Pattern 3, Continued: Can the Curve Be Changed?
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can evaluate whether geographic protection and alternative models shift value back toward producers.
Subject: Economics synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we drew the smile curve. Two quick questions.
One: what does the smile curve show? [Beat] Value capture is high at the two ends of a supply chain, branding at the start and marketing at the end, and lowest in the physical middle of farming and production (Mudambi, 2008).
Two: why do jimadores and rickhouse workers both sit at the bottom despite very different wages? [Beat] Because the curve measures share of the final value, not the size of the wage; both do physical middle work that captures a small slice of the price.

[HOOK] If the smile curve leaves the workers at the bottom, the next question is the one that matters. Can the shape be changed? Across the season we met two kinds of attempts: laws that fence off a region, and whole new models that route the money differently. They do not all work the same way.

[TEACH] Start with geographic protection, the rules that say a drink can only carry a name if it comes from a certain place. Season 2 gave us four, and their results are mixed. The French wine AOC of 1935 mostly helps established estates, because the barriers to entry favor whoever already holds the famous land. The United States bourbon standard of 1964 helps all U.S. distillers a moderate amount, because its national scale gives smaller players room. The Martinique rhum agricole AOC of 1996 helps small island producers well, because the island is small and the enforcement is coherent. And the Mexican tequila DO of 1974 is argued to favor large industrial producers, the framework captured by multinationals rather than the jimadores (Bowen, 2015). So a fence around a region can protect producers, but it can just as easily protect the biggest one inside the fence.
Now the second kind of attempt: models that change who owns what. A worker cooperative like South Africa's Thokozani gives workers equity, so they share in the profit, not only the wage. Direct trade and single-origin labeling in artisanal mezcal cut out the middleman and put a named producer on the bottle, so the buyer's premium reaches the maker. Fair-trade certification in coffee and chocolate sets a price floor and a community fund, a modest lift. And the strongest is the producer-owned brand, like Uncle Nearest, which reclaims the brand margin, the very top of the smile, for a community rather than an outside corporation (Ponte, 2002). That model has the highest potential to move value down the curve, because it does not just raise the wage at the bottom. It hands the producer the high corner of the smile.
So the honest answer is this. Geographic protection mostly redraws the fence, and value still pools where the brand power already is. The models that truly move value are the ones that change ownership, that let the people who make the drink also own the story and the margin. That leaves an open question worth sitting with: what would have to change for geographic protection to truly help the people at the bottom, and not just the largest producer behind the same legal wall?

[PICTURE] Picture the smile curve again, with the workers in the dip. Now picture two hands reaching in. One draws a fence around a region; the money inside still drifts to the biggest estate. The other lifts a worker from the dip and sets her on the high right corner, the brand corner, where she now owns the name on the bottle. Same curve. Two very different rescues.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the four geographic protections shifted value with mixed results, the Mexican tequila DO of 1974 argued to favor large producers while the Martinique rhum agricole AOC of 1996 helps small island producers well (Bowen, 2015); the models that move value most are ownership models, with producer-owned brands like Uncle Nearest holding the highest potential because they reclaim the brand margin itself (Ponte, 2002).

[CHECK] Quick check. Which kind of approach has the highest potential to move value down the curve, and why? [Beat] The producer-owned brand, such as Uncle Nearest, because it reclaims the brand margin at the top of the smile for the community rather than only lifting the wage at the bottom (Ponte, 2002).

## Key Takeaways
- Four geographic protections gave mixed results: the French wine AOC of 1935 helps established estates; the U.S. bourbon standard of 1964 helps all distillers moderately; the Martinique rhum agricole AOC of 1996 helps small island producers well; the Mexican tequila DO of 1974 is argued to favor large producers (Bowen, 2015).
- Geographic protection often redraws the fence without moving value to the bottom.
- Ownership models, worker cooperatives, direct trade, single-origin labeling, fair trade, and producer-owned brands, route value differently (Ponte, 2002).
- The producer-owned brand has the highest potential, because it reclaims the brand margin at the top of the curve.

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Ponte, S. (2002). The latte revolution? World Development, 30(7), 1099-1122.

title: Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can apply the four questions that test any marketing story.
Subject: English Language Arts synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we asked whether the smile curve can change. Two quick questions.
One: what does the smile curve show? [Beat] Value is captured at the ends of a supply chain and lowest in the physical middle (Mudambi, 2008).
Two: name one model that moves value toward producers. [Beat] Worker cooperatives, direct trade, single-origin labels, fair trade, or producer-owned brands like Uncle Nearest (Bowen, 2015).

[HOOK] Every drink this season came with two stories. One was printed on the label. The other was left off on purpose. And the power to decide which story survives is never neutral, it belongs to whoever has the most to gain.

[TEACH] Walk back through the season and the pattern is everywhere. Beer's label says craft heritage and since 1847, while the quieter story is mass consolidation, with a handful of companies controlling most of the market. Whiskey's label says small-batch craft with a founding tale, while many of those bottles are quietly sourced from one big Indiana plant and the rickhouse workers stay invisible. Rum's marketing sells a tropical paradise, while the suppressed story is the Triangular Trade and Maroon resistance. Mezcal's copy invokes ancient Indigenous tradition, while the jimador earns about twenty dollars a day. Sake's story is Japanese craftsmanship, while Korea's suppressed makgeolli history goes unmentioned. In every case, the marketing story protects someone's profit, and the suppressed story names a cost.
So Better Vice Club gives you a tool: four questions to ask of any drink's story. Who tells the story? Who benefits from this version? What can be verified, by a regulatory filing, peer-reviewed research, or a primary source? And what is constructed, meaning brand language or contested framing? Run any About Us page through those four questions and the gap between the polished story and the verifiable truth comes into focus. This is media literacy you can use on anything, not just a bottle.

[PICTURE] Picture a beautiful bottle with a warm story on the back label. Now picture a second label behind it, the one no one printed, listing who did the hard work, what was left out, and who profits from the version you are meant to believe. The four questions are how you read the second label.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: every marketing story has a suppressed counterpart, and the four questions, who tells it, who benefits, what can be verified, and what is constructed, let you test any drink's narrative for the gap between story and truth. The power to choose which story survives is never neutral.

[CHECK] Quick check. Name two of the four questions Better Vice Club asks of any marketing story. [Beat] Any two of: who tells the story, who benefits from this version, what can be verified, and what is constructed.

## Key Takeaways
- Every Season 2 drink had a marketing story and a suppressed story, and the marketing version protects someone's profit.
- The four questions: who tells the story, who benefits, what can be verified, and what is constructed.
- Examples run across the season, from whiskey's hidden sourcing to sake's unmentioned Korean history.
- The power to decide which story survives is never neutral, so these questions are media literacy for anything.

## Sources
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.

title: Pattern 4, Continued: What Gets Burned, What Gets Saved
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what lets a suppressed story survive and come back.
Subject: English Language Arts synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned the four questions. Two quick questions.
One: name two of the four questions for any marketing story. [Beat] Any two of who tells it, who benefits, what can be verified, and what is constructed.
Two: what does every marketing story have? [Beat] A suppressed counterpart that names a cost.

[HOOK] Some stories get burned. Some get saved. The difference is not luck. Across both seasons, the suppressed stories that came back shared two things, and the ones lost forever were missing one of them.

[TEACH] Start with what was nearly lost. The Spanish burned the Aztec codices so thoroughly that fewer than 20 pre-Columbian examples survive, and most of that knowledge is gone for good (Leon-Portilla, 2002). Korean makgeolli and its nuruk cultures were suppressed under Japanese occupation and came close to disappearing. The Maroon communities who freed themselves were written out of the official record. And Nathan Green, the enslaved distiller who taught Jack Daniel, vanished from the story for 130 years.
Now look at what came back. Korean brewers and scientists revived nuruk and recovered much of the lost microbial knowledge. Maroon histories were documented by scholars like C.L.R. James and Richard Price, working from oral tradition. And Nathan Green's story returned after a 2016 investigation, and is now carried by a whole whiskey brand. Each recovery needed two things: a community that kept the memory alive even without books or marketing, and someone with a platform who finally asked the question and did the work. Where both existed, the story could be saved. Where the community memory was destroyed too, as with most codices, the loss is permanent.
This is the hopeful half of the pattern. Erasure is powerful, but it is not always final. A story suppressed but not destroyed can still be recovered, because human memory is a durable medium.

[PICTURE] Picture two fires. In one, the codices burn and the smoke carries away knowledge no one can ever read again. In the other, a family keeps a story alive at the kitchen table for 130 years, until one day someone writes it down and the world finally hears it. Same flame of erasure, two different endings.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: a suppressed story survives when two things hold, a community keeps the memory alive and someone with a platform finally documents it, so makgeolli, Maroon histories, and Nathan Green's story came back, while most codex knowledge, with its memory destroyed too, is lost for good (Leon-Portilla, 2002). Erasure is powerful but not always final.

[CHECK] Quick check. What two things let a suppressed story be recovered? [Beat] A community keeping the memory alive, and someone with a platform who finally documents and verifies it (Leon-Portilla, 2002).

## Key Takeaways
- Many suppressed stories nearly vanished: the Aztec codices, Korean makgeolli, Maroon histories, and Nathan Green's role.
- Recovery needed two things: living community memory and someone with a platform who documented it.
- Makgeolli, Maroon histories, and Nathan Green's story came back; most codex knowledge is permanently lost (Leon-Portilla, 2002).
- Erasure is powerful but not always final, because human memory is a durable medium.

## Sources
- Leon-Portilla, M. (2002). The broken spears: The Aztec account of the conquest of Mexico (Rev. ed.). Beacon Press.

title: The Toast in Ten Languages
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what toasting traditions across many languages reveal, and hold the honest question they raise.
Subject: English Language Arts synthesis | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we asked what gets saved. Two quick questions.
One: what two things let a suppressed story survive? [Beat] Community memory and someone with a platform who documents it (Leon-Portilla, 2002).
Two: which season-long pattern is this part of? [Beat] Whose story survives, pattern four.

[HOOK] We end where people have ended for thousands of years: with a raised glass and a single word. And when you line up that word across ten languages, something quietly remarkable appears. Almost all of them say the same thing.

[TEACH] Listen to the toasts. In Spanish, salud, health. In Irish Gaelic, slainte, health. In French, sante, health. In Portuguese, saude, health. In Russian, na zdorovie, to health. In Hebrew, l'chaim, to life. That is six languages, from six different cultures, all wishing health or life. Two more describe the act itself: the Japanese kanpai and the Chinese gan bei both mean dry the glass, and the Scandinavian skal names the drinking bowl. Across ten languages and vastly different cultures, eight of ten toasts invoke health or life, and the act of raising a glass toward another person is coded, again and again, as a wish for their wellbeing.
Now hold the honest question, because this is a Better Vice Club class and we do not flinch. Is this convergence real evidence that fermented drinks and human connection are genuinely linked across cultures? Or is it evidence that alcohol cultures everywhere develop rituals to frame drinking as positive, a wish for health wrapped around a substance that, as we have said all season, also does real harm? Both readings are defensible. The convergence is real. What it means is yours to argue, with the evidence from the whole season behind you.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Ten cities scattered across the world, from Dublin to Tokyo to Jerusalem, each one a different word for the same gesture. Picture all of them raising a glass at once, eight of ten saying, in their own tongue, to your health, to your life.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: across ten languages, eight of ten toasts mean health or life, and the two others name the act of drinking, so raising a glass is coded almost universally as a wish for wellbeing, though whether that reflects genuine human connection or alcohol cultures framing themselves positively is a question worth arguing honestly. To your health, and to clear eyes about why we say it.

[CHECK] Quick check. Of ten toasting words across languages, what do most of them mean? [Beat] Most, eight of ten, mean health or life, like salud, slainte, and l'chaim.

## Key Takeaways
- Across ten languages, eight of ten toasts invoke health or life; two name the act of drinking.
- The convergence suggests something near-universal about raising a glass as a wish for wellbeing.
- The honest open question: is this genuine human connection, or alcohol cultures framing use as positive?
- Better Vice Club ends as it began, holding the craft and the cost of these drinks together.

## Sources
- World Health Organization. (2024). Global status report on alcohol and health. WHO Press. https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240089396

title: Key Terms: The Four Patterns
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core ideas of the four season-wide patterns.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the season finale. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Pattern 1: Geography Insists

**Universal terroir** (tehr-WAHR). The season's rule that geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, and fermentation shapes the drink (Gautier, 2018).

**Stress specialist.** A crop, like agave, that makes a better drink the harder its growing conditions are.

## Pattern 2: Fermentation Begins Sacred

**Sacred-to-commercial pattern.** The arc where a drink begins sacred, becomes regulated, then commercial, and harm rises (Civil, 1964).

**Communal accountability.** Social or sacred structures that governed who could drink and when, removed by commercial markets.

**Colonial disruption.** The suppression of Indigenous fermentation knowledge and practice by a colonizing power (Leon-Portilla, 2002).

## Pattern 3: Labor Captures the Smallest Share

**Smile curve.** A model where supply-chain value is high at the ends, branding and retail, and lowest in the physical middle (Mudambi, 2008).

**Value capture.** The share of a product's price that a given participant in the supply chain keeps.

**Geographic protection.** A legal designation, like an AOC or DO, meant to tie value to a place, with mixed results (Bowen, 2015).

**Worker cooperative.** A model where workers own equity and share profit, moving value toward labor.

## Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives

**Narrative analysis.** Testing a marketing story with four questions: who tells it, who benefits, what is verified, what is constructed.

**Suppressed narrative.** The alternative, often costly story that a marketing story leaves out.

**Cultural recovery.** Bringing back a suppressed tradition through surviving community memory and documentation (Leon-Portilla, 2002).

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). University of Chicago Press.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (2002). The broken spears: The Aztec account of the conquest of Mexico (Rev. ed.). Beacon Press.
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.

title: Cumulative Review: The Whole Season
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the four patterns and how they tie the whole season together.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull all of Season 2 together, one pattern at a time, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Pattern one, geography insists. Across beer, wine, whiskey, rum, agave spirits, and sake, one rule never broke: geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, and fermentation shapes the drink. The ground speaks first, the same lesson from Plzen's soft water to Niigata's snowmelt (Gautier, 2018). And the crops run a spectrum, from stress-loving agave to comfort-loving rice.

Pattern two, fermentation begins sacred. Nearly every drink started in a temple or ceremony, beer with Ninkasi, tequila with Mayahuel, sake with the kami, with rum the one exception (Civil, 1964). Sacred systems built communal accountability, and as drinks were commercialized and that accountability fell away, harm rose. Colonialism suppressed many traditions, the codices, makgeolli, Aztec ceremonies, and only some came back (Leon-Portilla, 2002).

Pattern three, labor captures the smallest share. The smile curve puts value at the ends of the supply chain, branding and retail, and leaves the physical middle with the least (Mudambi, 2008). The jimador, the cane cutter, the rickhouse worker all sit at the bottom, while alternative models like cooperatives and producer-owned brands try to shift value back down (Bowen, 2015).

Pattern four, whose story survives. Every drink had a marketing story and a suppressed one, and four questions, who tells it, who benefits, what is verified, what is constructed, let you tell them apart. A story suppressed but not destroyed can still be recovered, because human memory is durable (Leon-Portilla, 2002).

And we ended where humans always end, raising a glass, with eight of ten toasts across the world wishing health or life.

[REMEMBER] Here is the whole season in one breath. Four patterns run through every glass: the ground speaks first, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and the power to choose which story survives is never neutral. Once you see them in a drink, you will see them in coffee, in clothing, in everything you buy. That is the real toast: to clear eyes, and to your health.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Name all four season-wide patterns. [Beat] Geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives.

## Key Takeaways
- Pattern 1, geography insists: the ground shapes water and crop, which shape the drink (Gautier, 2018).
- Pattern 2, fermentation begins sacred: drinks start sacred, are commercialized, and harm rises as accountability falls (Civil, 1964; Leon-Portilla, 2002).
- Pattern 3, labor captures the smallest share: the smile curve leaves physical workers with the least (Mudambi, 2008).
- Pattern 4, whose story survives: marketing stories hide costs, and four questions reveal the truth.

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits. University of California Press.
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). University of Chicago Press.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (2002). The broken spears: The Aztec account of the conquest of Mexico (Rev. ed.). Beacon Press.
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.

title: Sources and Further Reading: The Toast
text_content:
This finale synthesizes all of Season 2, and every claim is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style, drawing on sources from across the season. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The season is educational, honest about alcohol's harms, and not an endorsement of drinking.

## References

title: Knowledge Check: The Toast
text_content:
This final check pulls from all four season-wide patterns: geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives. It rewards understanding the whole season, so having Episodes 8 to 13 fresh in your mind helps.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the capstone Project, where you apply all four patterns to a real beverage brand of your choice. That is the whole season, put to work.

Martinique AOC helped small island producers well, while the French AOC favors incumbents and the tequila DO is argued to favor large producers.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 3, Continued: Can the Curve Be Changed?"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"Which model has the highest potential to move value toward a community?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"A producer-owned brand like Uncle Nearest that reclaims the brand margin"},{"id":"q17b","text":"A multinational buyout"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Removing all labels"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Raising taxes only"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Producer-owned brands capture the brand margin for a community, the part of the curve where the most value sits, giving the highest potential to shift it.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 3, Continued: Can the Curve Be Changed?"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What are the four questions Better Vice Club asks of any marketing story?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Who tells it, who benefits, what can be verified, and what is constructed"},{"id":"q18b","text":"How much it costs, who buys it, where, and when"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Only whether it tastes good"},{"id":"q18d","text":"There are no such questions"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"These four questions test any narrative: who tells the story, who benefits from this version, what can be verified, and what is constructed.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What did the whiskey episode reveal as a suppressed story behind 'craft small-batch' marketing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Many such bourbons are sourced from one large Indiana plant, and rickhouse workers stay invisible"},{"id":"q19b","text":"All craft bourbon is made by hand"},{"id":"q19c","text":"There is no sourcing involved"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Whiskey has no marketing"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Behind many craft founding stories, the liquid is sourced from MGP in Indiana, and the rickhouse workers earn little and go unmentioned.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"What do most toasts across ten languages mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Health or life, like salud, slainte, and l'chaim"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Money"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Goodbye"},{"id":"q20d","text":"They mean nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Across ten languages, eight of ten toasts invoke health or life, while two name the act of drinking itself.","citation":"Lesson: The Toast in Ten Languages"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What honest question does the convergence of toasts raise?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Whether it reflects genuine human connection, or alcohol cultures framing drinking as positive"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Whether toasts are translated correctly"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Whether glasses are expensive"},{"id":"q21d","text":"There is no question"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"The convergence is real, but its meaning is debatable: genuine connection, or rituals that frame a harmful substance as a wish for health. Both readings are defensible.","citation":"Lesson: The Toast in Ten Languages"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What does it mean that the power to decide which story survives is never neutral?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"The marketing story usually protects someone's profit, while the suppressed story names a cost"},{"id":"q22b","text":"All stories are equally told"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Stories have no stakes"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Only true stories ever survive"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Whoever has the most to gain shapes which version is remembered, so the marketing story protects profit and the suppressed story names who paid.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"Which suppressed stories came back across the two seasons?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Korean makgeolli, Maroon histories, and Nathan Green's story"},{"id":"q23b","text":"None recovered"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Only beer's history"},{"id":"q23d","text":"All codices were recovered"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Makgeolli, Maroon histories, and Nathan Green's role all returned, because community memory survived and someone documented them. Most codex knowledge did not return.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4, Continued: What Gets Burned, What Gets Saved"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"Why is most Aztec codex knowledge permanently lost while makgeolli recovered?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"The codices' community memory was destroyed too, while makgeolli's memory survived to guide its revival"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Codices were never important"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Makgeolli was written down completely"},{"id":"q24d","text":"There is no difference"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Recovery needs surviving community memory; where both records and memory were destroyed, as with most codices, the loss is permanent.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4, Continued: What Gets Burned, What Gets Saved"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What are the four season-wide patterns?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Geography insists, fermentation begins sacred, labor captures the smallest share, and whose story survives"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Buy, sell, drink, repeat"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Only geography matters"},{"id":"q25d","text":"There are no patterns"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"The four patterns are the spine of the season, one for each lens: geography, social studies, economics, and language.","citation":"Lesson: The Toast (intro)"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"How does Pattern 1 connect Season 2 back to Season 1?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The same terroir rule that shaped sake and bourbon also shaped Season 1's coffee on volcanic slopes and tea at altitude"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Season 1 had no geography"},{"id":"q26c","text":"The seasons are unrelated"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Only Season 2 has terroir"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"The ground-speaks-first rule is the same across both seasons, from Ethiopian coffee and Darjeeling tea to Kentucky bourbon and Japanese sake.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 1: The Ground Speaks First"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"How did sacred regulatory systems shape drinking?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"They built communal accountability into drinking, like the Code of Hammurabi and Aztec religious law"},{"id":"q27b","text":"They encouraged unlimited drinking"},{"id":"q27c","text":"They had no rules"},{"id":"q27d","text":"They banned all fermentation"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Sacred systems regulated who could drink and when, embedding communal accountability that commercial markets later removed.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 2: Fermentation Begins Sacred"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What does the labor economics table reveal across both seasons?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"From coffee farmers to jimadores, the agricultural workers capture the smallest share of retail value"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Farmers earn the most"},{"id":"q28c","text":"There is no pattern in wages"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Only sake workers are paid"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Coffee farmers at a few dollars a day and jimadores at 15 to 25 dollars a day all sit at the bottom of the value curve, the consistent pattern.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 3: Labor Captures the Smallest Share"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What is a worker cooperative, as an alternative economic model?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"A model where workers own equity and share profit, like South Africa's Thokozani"},{"id":"q29b","text":"A government tax office"},{"id":"q29c","text":"A celebrity brand"},{"id":"q29d","text":"A distillery with no workers"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Worker cooperatives like Thokozani give workers ownership and profit-sharing, moving more value to the people who do the physical work.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 3, Continued: Can the Curve Be Changed?"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"Why does Better Vice Club end by analyzing the toast itself?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"The toast is itself a text, and its near-universal wish for health raises an honest question worth arguing"},{"id":"q30b","text":"To encourage drinking"},{"id":"q30c","text":"Because toasts have no meaning"},{"id":"q30d","text":"To sell more bottles"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"The toast across ten languages is a literary text, and its convergence on health invites the season's final honest debate about meaning and harm.","citation":"Lesson: The Toast in Ten Languages"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What is the geological mechanism behind every Season 2 water style?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Rain dissolves minerals from local rock, and those minerals shape the fermentation"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Brewers add minerals by law"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Water is always identical"},{"id":"q31d","text":"The climate adds the minerals directly"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"As water moves through rock, it picks up minerals, and the specific mineral profile shapes how fermentation proceeds and what style results.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 1: The Ground Speaks First"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What did colonial powers most often suppress in fermentation traditions?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Indigenous knowledge and community practice, from codices to nuruk to ceremonies"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Only the price of drinks"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Nothing of importance"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Their own traditions"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Across both seasons, colonial power suppressed Indigenous knowledge and communal practice, from burned codices to banned ceremonies to suppressed makgeolli.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 2, Continued: What Was Lost and What Came Back"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What makes the smile curve so visible in beverages specifically?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"The labor is intensely physical and the products are sold at high premiums"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Beverages have no labor"},{"id":"q33c","text":"They are all cheap"},{"id":"q33d","text":"The curve does not apply to drinks"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Because the harvesting and production work is so physical and the bottles so premium, the gap between who works and who profits is starkly visible.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 3: Labor Captures the Smallest Share"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What does the season say about erasure?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Erasure is powerful but not always final, because human memory is a durable medium"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Erasure always succeeds completely"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Erasure never happens"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Erasure only affects drinks"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"A story suppressed but not destroyed can still be recovered, because communities can carry memory across generations until someone documents it.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4, Continued: What Gets Burned, What Gets Saved"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Which pattern would you use to read a brand's About Us page critically?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Pattern 4, whose story survives, with its four questions"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Pattern 1, the water rule"},{"id":"q35c","text":"No pattern applies"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Only the smile curve"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The four questions of Pattern 4, who tells it, who benefits, what is verified, and what is constructed, are media-literacy tools for any marketing text.","citation":"Lesson: Pattern 4: Whose Story Survives"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What is the spirit of Better Vice Club's honesty, start to finish?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Holding the craft and the cost of these drinks together, never flinching from either"},{"id":"q36b","text":"Promoting drinking as healthy"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Ignoring all harm"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Only celebrating the products"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"The season studies these drinks as history, geography, economics, and culture while staying honest about their real harms, holding both at once.","citation":"Lesson: The Toast (intro)"}]}
